title: Study to learn, then ace the test
description: Practice like the test, then read questions like a detective.
summaryPoints: Quiz yourself with practice problems and past papers — recognition isn't knowing
Mix problem types and space practice across days instead of cramming
Read every question carefully and watch for words like 'not', 'all', and 'except'
Budget your time: easy first, flag the hard one, answer everything, then check
Treat a returned test as a free study guide — review every miss and find why

content.title: Re-reading is a trap
content.body:
You highlight the notes. You read them again. It all looks familiar, so you feel ready.

Then the exam asks you to actually do the thing, and your brain goes blank.

That gap has a name: recognizing something is not the same as knowing it. The fix isn't reading harder — it's switching from study-to-recognize to study-to-**learn**.

content.title: Test yourself, not your notes
content.body:
The single most powerful study tool is **practice testing**: pulling answers out of your head instead of rereading them in.

Do practice problems and past papers under test-like conditions — phone away, timer on. It builds memory AND makes the real exam feel familiar.

Solve enough that on test day you think: wait, I've basically seen this one before.
content.imageAlt: A student taking a calm deep breath before an exam

content.prompt: Re-reading your notes until they feel familiar is the best way to prepare.
content.explanation: Familiar isn't the same as known. Pulling answers from memory with practice problems beats rereading every time.

content.prompt: Which study tool actually builds memory the most?
options: Reading the chapter a third time
content.explanation: Retrieving answers under test-like conditions strengthens memory and makes the real exam familiar. The other three just feel productive.

items.text: Doing a past paper with a timer
Quizzing yourself with the book closed

content.title: Mix it up, spread it out
content.body:
Two upgrades make practice stick:

**Interleave** — mix problem types instead of drilling 20 of the same kind in a row. Switching forces you to figure out which method fits, the exact skill the test demands.

**Space** — spread practice across days. Cramming feels productive at 1 a.m., but it fades fast. A little today, a little in two days, a little next week beats one giant night.

content.instructions: One word makes this advice backwards. Tap it.
content.sentence: Spreading study across several days works worse than one big cram.
content.explanation: Spaced practice beats cramming — the same hours spread out stick far longer.

content.prompt: Match each study strategy to why it works.

content.title: Plan backward from the date
content.body:
Don't plan from today forward — plan from the exam backward.

Start at the test date and walk back: what do you want to know cold the night before? The week before? Now? That tells you what to practice today, and it spaces your sessions automatically.

A returned or practice test you bombed isn't a verdict. It's a map of exactly what to hit next.

content.title: Test day: be a detective
content.body:
Walk in calm, then read like a detective.

- Read the instructions and **each question** carefully
- Hunt for trap words: **not**, **except**, **always**, **all**
- Do easy questions first to bank points
- Hit a brutal one? Flag it, move on, come back
- On multiple-choice, eliminate the obviously wrong options
- Answer everything (unless wrong answers cost points), show your work, leave time to check

content.context: Question 4 is brutal. You've spent six minutes and you're stuck, with 25 questions to go.
options: Keep grinding until you crack it
Flag it, move on, come back later
Guess randomly and never return to it
Leave the whole rest of the section blank
content.explanation: Don't pour all your time into one question. Bank the easy points first, then return to the hard one with whatever time is left.

content.prompt: Put a smart test-day routine in order.
steps: Read the instructions and each question carefully
Flag the hard ones and come back to them
Use leftover time to check your work
content.explanation: Read first so you don't misread, bank easy points, circle back to the hard ones, then check before you hand it in.

content.prompt: On multiple-choice you don't know, first ___ the options that are clearly wrong.
content.hint: Cut the obvious losers to boost your odds.
content.explanation: Eliminating wrong options turns a wild guess into a much better one — fewer choices, better odds.

content.prompt: Smart test-day habit or weak one?

items.text: Showing your work as you go
Sinking all your time into question one
Leaving time at the end to check

content.title: A graded test is a gift
content.body:
Most people glance at the score and shove the test in a bag. Huge waste.

A returned test is a free study guide for the next one. Go through every miss and ask why: careless slip? misread the question? or genuinely didn't know it?

Then look for **patterns** — and redo the problems you got wrong, from scratch, until they're easy.

content.prompt: How should you use a returned test?
steps: Look at every question you got wrong
Figure out WHY you missed each one
Redo the missed problems from scratch
content.explanation: Finding the why turns mistakes into targets, and redoing them from scratch is the practice testing that actually fixes the gap.

content.prompt: Your exam is in about two weeks. Tap the review days that beat forgetting.
content.topic: Material for the exam in 2 weeks
content.explanation: Expanding intervals (today, +2, +5, +9) keep resetting the forgetting curve so it sticks. Cramming the night before alone doesn't.
